section.title: The Judge of the Earth
section.sectionRationale: An imprecatory psalm indicting corrupt human rulers who pervert justice, described as estranged from the womb and venomous as a deaf serpent. David prays boldly for God to break their power, and the psalm closes not in personal vengeance but in the settled conviction that there is a God who judges the earth.
section.thesis: Against corrupt rulers who pervert justice from birth, David prays boldly for God to break their power, confident that history will end with one settled verdict: there is a God who judges the earth, a verdict finally and fully rendered at the return of Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the indictment of corrupt judges who speak injustice; the depth of the wicked's corruption, pictured as a deaf, venomous serpent; the bold imprecatory prayer for God to break their power; and the closing vindication, surely there is a God who judges the earth.

units.label: Do you indeed speak justly, O rulers

verses.text: Do you indeed speak justly, O rulers? Do you judge uprightly, O sons of men?
No, in your hearts you devise {{avel|injustice}}; with your hands you mete out violence on the earth.

units.label: Estranged from the womb
units.note: the depth of the wicked's corruption

verses.text: The wicked are estranged from the womb; the liars go astray from birth.
Their venom is like the venom of a snake, like a cobra that shuts its ears,
refusing to hear the tune of the charmer who skillfully weaves his spell.
O God, shatter their teeth in their mouths; O LORD, tear out the fangs of the lions.
May they vanish like water that runs off; when they draw the bow, may their arrows be blunted.

Like a slug that dissolves in its slime, like a woman’s stillborn child, 
may they never see the sun.

Before your pots can feel the burning thorns, whether green or dry, 
He will sweep them away.

units.label: Surely there is a God who judges the earth

verses.text: The righteous will rejoice when they see they are avenged; they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked.

Then men will say, “There is surely a reward for the righteous! 
There is surely a {{shaphat|God who judges}} the earth!”

avel.gloss: Injustice, unrighteousness, moral wrong. The rulers indicted here have devised avel in their very hearts before ever acting, showing that the corruption of unjust judgment begins internally, in intention, before it is carried out in violence.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, to govern, to render a verdict. The psalm's closing declaration, there is surely a God who judges the earth, uses this same root that describes human rulers in verse 1, contrasting their failed, corrupt judgment with God's own perfect and final judgment.

basic.context: This psalm confronts corrupt human rulers directly, those entrusted with justice who instead devise violence in their hearts. David describes their corruption as running so deep it seems present from birth, like the venom of a snake that refuses even the charmer's skill. He then prays boldly, even violently, for God to break their power, using vivid images of broken teeth, melting slugs, and stillborn children. The psalm closes not in personal triumph but in a settled theological conclusion: there is a God who judges the earth.
basic.hermeneutics: This is an imprecatory psalm, and its harsh language should be read as honest, Spirit-inspired prayer appealing to God's own justice against real, entrenched corruption in those who hold power, not as personal vengeance for private grievance. The psalmist does not act against the wicked himself; he hands the whole matter to God, the only one with the right and the ability to judge the earth justly.
basic.originalLanguages: The rulers indicted in verse 2 devise avel, injustice, internally, in their hearts, before it ever surfaces in violent action. The psalm's closing declaration uses shaphat, judge, the very verb describing the corrupt human rulers of verse 1, contrasting their failed judgment with God's perfect and final verdict over all the earth.
basic.typology: The psalm's demand that corrupt judges be finally overturned and that a righteous verdict be rendered over the whole earth anticipates the final judgment, when Christ, the only truly righteous Judge, will set every wrong right and vindicate the oppressed completely.
basic.christ: This psalm's imprecations are not personal revenge but appeals to God as the only righteous Judge, a posture the New Testament commends explicitly, leave room for God's wrath, for it is written, vengeance is Mine, I will repay, says the Lord, Romans 12:19. The psalm's closing hope, there is surely a God who judges the earth, is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, to whom the Father has given all authority to judge, and who has fixed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31, finally silencing every unjust mouth and vindicating every oppressed soul, Revelation 20:11-15.
basic.god: God is shown as the only truly righteous Judge, in stark contrast to corrupt human rulers who pervert justice from birth. His judgment, though the psalm prays for it urgently and vividly, is not capricious but perfectly aligned with true righteousness, and it will be rendered with certainty over the whole earth.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12:19 instructs believers to leave vengeance to God rather than take it themselves. Acts 17:31 declares that God has fixed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness through Jesus Christ. Revelation 20:11-15 pictures the final judgment before God's great white throne, when every unjust deed is finally accounted for.
basic.oneStory: Against corrupt rulers who twist justice from birth, this psalm prays boldly that God alone would judge and set things right, closing in the settled conviction that there is a God who judges the earth. That conviction is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, given all authority to judge, who will finally and completely vindicate the righteous and silence every unjust mouth.

context.literary: An imprecatory psalm of communal lament, moving from an indictment of corrupt rulers, verses 1-2, through a description of the wicked's inborn corruption, verses 3-5, a bold imprecatory prayer using vivid, cascading images of judgment, verses 6-9, to a closing declaration of vindication, verses 10-11.
context.historical: Likely arising from a specific situation of judicial corruption in David's era, where those entrusted with rendering justice instead used their position for violence and injustice, a perennial danger to any community whose leaders abandon righteousness.
context.canonical: Belongs among the imprecatory psalms, alongside Psalms 35, 69, and 109, which give voice to legitimate longing for God's justice against entrenched, unrepentant evil, always entrusting the outcome to God rather than to personal vengeance.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To indict corrupt rulers who pervert justice, to pray boldly for God's decisive intervention against their entrenched wickedness, and to affirm confidently that God will finally and rightly judge the whole earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific situation of judicial corruption; it models a pattern still valid for believers, honest, even vivid, lament and prayer for God's justice against entrenched evil, entrusted entirely to God rather than acted out personally.

debates.question: How should Christians read violent imprecatory language like verses 6-9, given the New Testament's call to love enemies?

views.label: As honest, Spirit-inspired prayer appealing to God's justice, not a model for personal action
views.case: The psalm never calls on David himself to act against the wicked; every verb of judgment is addressed to God, consistent with entrusting vengeance to Him rather than seeking it personally, which the New Testament also commends, Romans 12:19.

views.label: As covenantal language appropriate to a theocratic, national context, not directly transferable to personal relationships today
views.case: David speaks as a covenant king within Israel's unique theocratic structure, addressing corrupt judges with covenant responsibilities; while the substance of trusting God's justice remains applicable, the specific vivid imagery reflects that particular ancient Near Eastern political and covenantal setting.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm never authorizes personal vengeance; it consistently directs the desire for justice toward God alone, a pattern fully consistent with loving one's enemies while still longing for true justice to be done.

study.originalLanguages: The rulers indicted in verse 2 devise avel, injustice, internally in their hearts before it surfaces in violent action. The psalm's closing declaration uses shaphat, judge, the same verb describing the corrupt human rulers of verse 1, contrasting their failed judgment with God's perfect and final verdict over the whole earth.

typology.type: The demand for a righteous verdict to finally overturn corrupt human judgment, verses 1-2, 10-11
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment, when Christ, the only truly righteous Judge, will set every wrong right, Acts 17:31, Revelation 20:11-15.

study.christ: This psalm's imprecations are appeals to God as the only righteous Judge, not personal revenge, a posture the New Testament commends explicitly, leave room for God's wrath, Romans 12:19. The psalm's closing hope, there is surely a God who judges the earth, is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, to whom the Father has given all authority to judge, and who has fixed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31, finally silencing every unjust mouth and vindicating every oppressed soul, Revelation 20:11-15.
study.god: God is the only truly righteous Judge, in stark contrast to corrupt human rulers who pervert justice from birth. His judgment, though prayed for here urgently and vividly, is never capricious but perfectly aligned with true righteousness, and it will be rendered with certainty over the whole earth.

crossReferences.note: Believers are instructed to leave vengeance to God rather than take it themselves.
God has fixed a day on which He will judge the world in righteousness through Jesus Christ.
The final judgment before God's great white throne, when every unjust deed is finally accounted for.

study.oneStory: Against corrupt rulers who twist justice from birth, this psalm prays boldly that God alone would judge and set things right, closing in the settled conviction that there is a God who judges the earth. That conviction is fulfilled in Jesus Christ, given all authority to judge, who will finally and completely vindicate the righteous and silence every unjust mouth.

quiz.q: Who does this psalm indict in its opening verses?

options.text: Rulers and judges who devise injustice and violence rather than judging uprightly.
options.note: Verses 1-2 directly address rulers who fail to speak justly or judge uprightly.

options.text: Foreign invading armies.
options.note: The indictment is against corrupt rulers or judges, not a foreign invading force.

options.text: Ordinary farmers and merchants.
options.note: The psalm's target is specifically those entrusted with rendering justice, not ordinary tradespeople.

options.text: Prophets who spoke false messages.
options.note: The indictment concerns rulers and judges, not prophetic figures specifically.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the depth of the wicked's corruption in verses 3-5?

options.text: As estranged from the womb, going astray from birth, like a snake that refuses to hear the charmer.
options.note: This vivid language describes corruption as deep-seated and resistant to correction, present from birth.

options.text: As a temporary lapse in otherwise good judgment.
options.note: The text describes deep, persistent corruption, not a minor or temporary lapse.

options.text: As caused entirely by poor education.
options.note: The psalm's imagery describes an inborn moral condition, not merely a lack of education.

options.text: As limited to only a few isolated individuals.
options.note: The description applies broadly to 'the wicked' as a category, not merely a rare few individuals.

quiz.q: How should the vivid imprecatory prayers of verses 6-9 be understood?

options.text: As honest, Spirit-inspired prayer appealing to God's own justice, not a model for personal vengeance.
options.note: Every verb of judgment in these verses is directed to God to act, not to David himself.

options.text: As direct instructions for believers to physically harm their enemies.
options.note: The prayers are addressed entirely to God to act; they are not instructions for personal violence.

options.text: As purely poetic language with no real theological content.
options.note: The prayers reflect a genuine theological conviction about God's justice, not merely empty poetic flourish.

options.text: As evidence that David had abandoned faith in God's justice.
options.note: The opposite is true; these prayers reflect deep faith that God alone can and will render justice.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing conclusion in verse 11?

options.text: There is surely a reward for the righteous, and a God who judges the earth.
options.note: This is the psalm's climactic, settled theological conclusion.

options.text: There is no hope for justice in this life or the next.
options.note: The psalm concludes with confident hope in God's justice, not despair.

options.text: The righteous must take justice into their own hands.
options.note: The psalm consistently entrusts judgment to God, not to human self-action.

options.text: Corrupt rulers will always escape judgment.
options.note: The psalm's conclusion is the opposite: God will judge, and the righteous will see it.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's longing for justice find its fulfillment in the New Testament?

options.text: In Jesus Christ, to whom the Father has given all authority to judge the world in righteousness.
options.note: Acts 17:31 directly ties final, righteous judgment of the whole earth to Jesus Christ.

options.text: In the complete abolition of any future judgment.
options.note: The New Testament affirms a coming final judgment, not its abolition.

options.text: In teaching that all human rulers are equally righteous.
options.note: This is not the New Testament's teaching; corrupt human authority remains a real problem addressed by final divine judgment.

options.text: In encouraging believers to personally overthrow unjust rulers by force.
options.note: The New Testament, like this psalm, entrusts final judgment to God, not to violent personal or political overthrow.

questions: This psalm indicts leaders who use their position for injustice rather than righteousness. Where do you see corrupted power in the world today, and how does this psalm shape the way you pray about it?

The psalm describes the wicked's corruption as deep-seated, resistant even to skilled correction. How does this sober picture of sin's depth shape your understanding of your own need for God's grace, not just moral effort?

This psalm's harsh prayers are directed entirely to God to act, never instructing David to take vengeance himself. Where do you need to consciously hand a desire for justice or revenge over to God rather than acting on it yourself?
The psalm closes with confidence that there is a God who judges the earth. How does trusting in a final, certain judgment change the way you respond to injustice you cannot currently fix?
Jesus is given all authority to judge the world in righteousness. How does knowing that final judgment belongs entirely to Christ free you from needing to personally settle every score in this life?

translationNotes.note: Some translations render 'O rulers' as 'O gods' or 'O mighty ones,' reflecting an ambiguous Hebrew term (elem) that may address human judges functioning in a God-given role of authority, or possibly divine council imagery; either reading points to those entrusted with rendering justice.
Like a woman's stillborn child is a stark ancient Near Eastern image for something that never reaches its intended purpose or maturity, applied here to the wicked's schemes coming to nothing.

Wash their feet in the blood of the wicked is hyperbolic ancient Near Eastern imagery for a decisive, total victory and vindication, not a literal prescription for believers' conduct toward the defeated.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 58:1-11. Two em dashes present in the raw BSB text at verse 9 were converted to commas. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) confirmed for Psalm 58 this session; sermon status set to none.
